summary.overview: Indiana is in the midst of a severe utility affordability crisis, with residential electric bills rising an average of 17.5% in one year—the sharpest increase in two decades—driven by major rate hikes approved for Duke Energy Indiana, NIPSCO, AES Indiana, and CenterPoint Energy. In response, the IURC launched a historic investigative inquiry in February 2026, summoning the state's five largest investor-owned utilities to explain rising costs, and Gov. Mike Braun signed HEA 1002 into law, introducing performance-based ratemaking and new low-income protections. Despite these reforms, over 122,000 Indiana households depend on LIHEAP/EAP assistance that faces federal funding uncertainty, and consumer advocates warn that recent legislative changes do not go far enough to provide immediate relief for struggling Hoosiers.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households enrolled in EAP/LIHEAP
messaging_recommendations.message: Your utility bills should never force you to choose between heat and groceries—know your rights under Indiana's winter disconnection moratorium, new summer shutoff protections under HEA 1002, and automatic enrollment in levelized billing plans, and contact your local Community Action Agency if you need emergency help.

Hoosiers have experienced the steepest electric bill increases in 20 years, with some families paying $50 more per month than just a year ago—performance-based ratemaking (HEA 1002) is a first step, but trackers like TDSIC and CWIP must also be reformed, and the IURC must aggressively enforce its new affordability mandate rather than rubber-stamp future rate hike settlements.

Indiana's five investor-owned utilities requested billions in rate increases while reporting record profits, and the state's own consumer watchdog found that utilities like AES Indiana and NIPSCO had not demonstrated sufficient evidence for their requested hikes—Hoosiers deserve transparent billing, accountable regulators, and rates that are actually just and reasonable.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Corporate and industrial stakeholders and data center developers
messaging_recommendations.message: Large new electricity loads from data centers and AI infrastructure must not be subsidized by residential ratepayers who are already struggling; Indiana's new NIPSCO GenCo model and HEA 1002 provisions offer a framework to ringfence large-load costs, and advocates will hold regulators accountable to that standard.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana sought a two-phase rate increase of approximately $491.5 million to fund grid modernization and coal ash remediation. The IURC approved a reduced increase of approximately $295.7 million in annual revenues, implemented in Step 1 (~8% in February 2025) and Step 2 (~3% in early 2026); the OUCC and industrial customers subsequently alleged Duke overbilled customers by $89 million in unauthorized Step 2 charges and are seeking refunds.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately 11% overall (~8% Step 1, ~3% Step 2); $295.7 million annual revenue increase approved

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed for a rate review on June 3, 2025, requesting an annual incremental revenue increase of approximately $192.9 million, with implementation proposed in two phases—approximately 7.5% in Q2 2026 and 6% in January 2027. A partial settlement was reached in October 2025 reducing the proposed increase by more than half; the OUCC and CAC oppose the settlement and the case is fully briefed with an IURC final order deadline of June 24, 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Originally ~13.5% over two phases; settlement would reduce the amount; final order due June 24, 2026

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO sought an annual electric revenue increase of approximately $368.7 million to fund renewable energy investments and infrastructure modernization. A settlement reduced the increase to approximately $257 million, which the IURC approved on June 26, 2025; rates are being phased in from July 2025 through Q1 2026, resulting in an average residential increase of approximately $23/month (16.75%).
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately 16.75% average residential increase ($23/month); $257 million annual revenue increase approved

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy Indiana filed for a $118.8 million (16.02%) base electric rate increase for its southwestern Indiana service territory. The IURC approved approximately $80 million in February 2025, to be implemented in phases: Phase 1 in early 2025, Phase 2 in early 2026, resulting in an overall bill increase of approximately 20% for residential customers.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately 20% overall increase; ~$80 million approved; phased with Phase 2 taking effect in early 2026

rate_cases.utility: IURC (All Five Major Indiana Investor-Owned Utilities)
rate_cases.case_number: Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability (no formal Cause number assigned as of May 2026)
rate_cases.description: In February 2026, IURC Chair Andy Zay announced a formal investigative inquiry into energy affordability, summoning Duke Energy Indiana, AES Indiana, NIPSCO, CenterPoint Energy, and Indiana Michigan Power (I&M) to a March 24, 2026 hearing on billing transparency, usage drivers, and short- and long-term rate relief. The IURC followed with a statewide affordability listening tour through April 2026.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: IURC Chair Andy Zay announced a formal investigative inquiry into energy affordability, saying the balance between utilities and customers feels 'dramatically out of whack' for many Hoosiers. The commission summoned Indiana's five largest investor-owned electric utilities to a March 24, 2026, hearing to discuss billing transparency and potential rate relief. The inquiry came in response to growing customer outrage over rapidly rising energy bills across the state.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: In a daylong IURC hearing on March 24, 2026, Indiana's five largest utilities—Duke, NIPSCO, AES Indiana, CenterPoint, and Indiana Michigan Power—defended their rates and business practices before commissioners. Bipartisan concern over the pending acquisition of AES Indiana by foreign investors featured prominently in the proceedings. IURC Chair Andy Zay signaled that action from the commission would come within 30 to 60 days.

articles.headline: Braun signs HEA 1002 to lower Indiana energy costs
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun signed HEA 1002 into law, landmark legislation overhauling how Indiana regulates utilities by introducing performance-based ratemaking tied to measurable affordability and reliability benchmarks. The law replaces a model that had effectively guaranteed utility returns on equity regardless of customer outcomes. It also creates new low-income bill stabilization programs, a summer disconnection moratorium, and requirements for greater billing transparency.

articles.headline: Rising energy bills spark investigation by Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission
articles.summary: WSBT reported that NIPSCO customers using 1,000 kWh in July 2025 saw over a 90% increase in their bill compared to 2016, prompting State Rep. Jim Pressel and 15 fellow northern Indiana legislators to sign a letter demanding the IURC investigate NIPSCO's rates. The IURC announced its broader five-utility investigative inquiry the same week. NIPSCO stated it was aware of the inquiry and committed to cooperating with regulators.

articles.headline: Indiana representative calling for review of NIPSCO rates
articles.summary: State Rep. Jim Pressel (R-Rolling Prairie) called on the IURC to review NIPSCO's increasing residential utility rates, noting constituents have seen their bills surge over 90% since 2016. Pressel was later joined by 15 other northern Indiana legislators co-signing a letter to IURC Chair Andy Zay demanding a formal review. The article highlighted IURC survey data and U.S. Energy Information Administration data both confirming NIPSCO's outsized rate increases.

articles.headline: IURC holding 'energy affordability' listening sessions
articles.summary: Following its March 24 investigative hearing, the IURC announced a statewide listening tour to directly hear from utility customers about affordability challenges. Sessions were scheduled from late March through April 2026, with IURC Chair Zay inviting Hoosiers to bring copies of their utility bills. The tour represents the second phase of the IURC's informal investigation into Indiana's affordability crisis.

articles.headline: Your electric bill is going to change. New law aims to stabilize utilities
articles.summary: WFYI explained the provisions of HEA 1002, including a new summer disconnection moratorium for the hottest days (when the National Weather Service forecasts extreme heat) and a prohibition against utilities passing lobbying costs to ratepayers. The law also mandates that starting after June 30, 2026, eligible low-income households enrolled in EAP will be automatically moved to levelized billing plans. Critics noted that several stronger consumer-protection amendments were voted down along party lines.

articles.headline: State agency recommends lowering AES rates
articles.summary: The Indiana OUCC recommended in September 2025 that the IURC not only deny AES Indiana's proposed rate increase but actually decrease its current rates, after a three-month legal and technical review. AES Indiana's proposed increase of 13.5% over two years would add about $21/month for a typical residential customer. The OUCC stated that AES had not demonstrated sufficient evidence for the increase, filed less than 14 months after its last rate case approval.

articles.headline: Hoosiers' electric bills spike 17.5% in one year
articles.summary: A Citizens Action Coalition analysis found that average residential electric bills in Indiana surged 17.5% in the 12-month period ending July 2025—the most severe increase in at least 20 years. NIPSCO residential customers were hit hardest, with a $50/month (26.7%) increase in a single year, compounding a 17.8% hike the prior year. The findings intensified pressure on state regulators and legislators to act.

articles.headline: Consumer advocates oppose proposed AES Indiana rate settlement
articles.summary: Consumer advocates including the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition publicly opposed a partial settlement in AES Indiana's rate case (Cause No. 46258), arguing it favors large commercial and industrial customers over residential ratepayers. The settlement would result in a 6.51% base rate increase for residential customers, while commercial and industrial customers would see only 3.19% and 4.12% increases, respectively. A final IURC order is expected by June 24, 2026.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: That balance feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers and today is the beginning of a process that we are labeling an investigative inquiry. We're asking our five investor-owned utilities that we regulate on a week-in, week-out basis to come to this room and have an open dialogue about affordability.
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Daily Journal news conference coverage

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: In my short time as chairman of the IURC, my fellow Commissioners and I have heard from thousands of utility customers from all over Indiana that they are struggling to pay their energy bills.
stakeholders.source: Daily Journal — IURC affordability listening sessions announcement

stakeholders.name: Jim Pressel
stakeholders.title: State Representative (R-Rolling Prairie)
stakeholders.quote: I was a little disappointed frankly with the responses from NIPSCO. Didn't seem like we were getting answers to the questions that the commissioners were asking. I was really disappointed that we didn't hear something on how can we lower rates.
stakeholders.source: WNDU — Indiana regulators question utility companies on rising electric bills

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor (Indiana's 25th)
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving Commission approval in its last rate case. My team is recommending additional reductions that would reduce the utility's current rates and help lessen the burden on ratepayers. (Paraphrased from OUCC press release, Sept. 10, 2025)
stakeholders.source: OUCC Press Release, Cause No. 46258, September 10, 2025

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: We have a deep commitment to operating efficiently and keeping rates as low as possible. Throughout this process, we've listened to stakeholder feedback and evaluated tradeoffs we will make for future investments while minimizing the financial impact of delivering safe and reliable electric service.
stakeholders.source: AES Indiana press release / Daily Journal — Consumer advocates oppose proposed AES Indiana rate settlement

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: For more than a century, AES Indiana has reliably served the needs of customers across Central Indiana. Today, in 2025, the cost to deliver the essential service our customers depend on continues to rise.
stakeholders.source: AES Indiana press release — AES Indiana seeks rate review to address rising operational costs

stakeholders.name: Megan Robertson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: Hoosier families are stretched thin, and they need relief now from monopoly utilities who've pushed policies for years that led to this crisis.
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition news release — ICV, CAC Unveil Policy Plan to Lower Energy Costs for Hoosiers

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: Affordability is this administration's top priority, and high utility bills are one of the biggest affordability challenges facing Hoosier families.
stakeholders.source: WNDU — Indiana Gov. Mike Braun signs bill aimed at lowering utility costs

stakeholders.name: Phil GiaQuinta
stakeholders.title: State Representative (D-Fort Wayne)
stakeholders.quote: No home runs, nothing that really kind of knocked it out of the park when it comes to what can we do to help people that are struggling with their utility bills and just the overall cost of living.
stakeholders.source: WFYI — 2026 session wrap: Did Indiana lawmakers deliver on affordability?
